101	functor"; Stacks tag 01HK). -/
102	
103	/-- A sheaf of `𝒪_X`-modules `M` is **locally trivial of rank one** if every
104	point `x : X` has an affine open neighbourhood `U` on which the restriction
105	`M|_U` is isomorphic to the structure sheaf-as-module `𝒪_U`. This is the
106	project-side stand-in for the missing Mathlib `IsInvertible` predicate on
107	`Scheme.Modules`: line bundles (invertible sheaves) are exactly the
108	`IsLocallyTrivial` modules, by Stacks tag 01HK / Hartshorne II §6. Local
109	trivialisability is the structural content that survives base change along
110	`f : Y ⟶ X` (Stacks 01HH) and is the source of the invertibility-preservation
111	step for `pullbackAlongProjection`.
112	
113	The witness is bundled as `Nonempty (M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit _)`:
114	the existence of a sheaf-of-modules isomorphism over the affine chart. -/
115	def IsLocallyTrivial {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) : Prop :=
116	  ∀ x : X, ∃ U : X.Opens, x ∈ U ∧ IsAffineOpen U ∧
117	    Nonempty (M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf)

35	- **Mathlib idiom — RING level only, COMPLETE.** `Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup` gives, from
36	  `Module.Invertible R M`:
37	  - `Module.Invertible.instProjective` (⟹ `Module.Projective R M`),
38	  - `Module.Invertible.instFinite` (⟹ `Module.Finite R M`),
39	  - `Module.Invertible.finrank_eq_one` (over a free/local base, `finrank = 1`),
40	  - `Module.Invertible.of_isLocalization` (base-change stability),
41	  - `CommRing.Pic.mk_eq_one_iff_free`.
42	  Composing `instProjective + instFinite` with `Module.finitePresentation_of_projective`
43	  (`Mathlib.Algebra.Module.FinitePresentation`) gives `Module.FinitePresentation R M`, i.e. the
44	  full "invertible ⟹ finite projective rank-1" at the level of a module over a **ring**.
45	- **Sheaf-level coherence predicates EXIST but are NOT connected to invertibility.** Present:
46	  `SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType` (`…Sheaf.Generators`), `SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation`
47	  / `QuasicoherentData.IsFinitePresentation` (`…Sheaf.Quasicoherent`),
48	  `SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent`, `SheafOfModules.free` / `ιFree` (`…Sheaf.Free`),
49	  `instIsFiniteTypeOfIsFinitePresentation`.
50	- **Verified ABSENT** (LSP: no loogle/leansearch hit):
51	  1. NO `SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFree` / `Scheme.Modules.IsLocallyFree` predicate at all.
52	  2. NO "invertible sheaf ⟹ locally free rank 1" at `SheafOfModules` / `Scheme.Modules` level.
53	  3. NO map from the sheaf-level `IsInvertible` (`∃N, M⊗N≅𝒪`) to ANY coherence predicate
54	     (`IsFiniteType`/`IsFinitePresentation`): `IsInvertible` supplies **no finite-presentation
55	     datum** for `M` as a sheaf.
56	  4. NO stalk-iso ⟹ neighbourhood-iso spreading-out for `SheafOfModules`. (Project's
57	     `isIso_of_isIso_restrict` is global-from-a-GIVEN-cover, not single-stalk spread-out.)
58	- **Gap**: divergent-with-cost at sheaf level. **Verdict: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL** (sheaf level).
59	  Mathlib has the ring-level half complete and the sheaf-level coherence *vocabulary*, but not the
60	  glue.
61	
62	### Decision 2: Same Mathlib-scale spreading-out as the forward bridge, or cheaper? (Q2)
63	
64	The statement **factors**, and the two halves have completely different cost:
65	
66	- **HARD half — `IsInvertible M ⟹ M finitely presented (loc-triv)`.** This IS the same
67	  Mathlib-absent finite-presentation spreading-out that iter-245 priced for the forward bridge
68	  `IsInvertible ⟹ IsLocallyTrivial`. To realise it you must: pass to a stalk `𝒪_{X,x}`, show
69	  `M_x` invertible over the local ring (⟹ free rank 1 via `Module.Invertible` + `finrank_eq_one`),
70	  THEN spread the stalk-freeness to a Zariski neighbourhood. The spreading-out engine
71	  (`Module.isOpen_freeLocus`, `Module.basicOpen_subset_freeLocus_iff`,
72	  `Module.freeLocus_eq_univ`, all in `…Spectrum.Prime.FreeLocus`) **requires
73	  `[Module.FinitePresentation R M]` as a hypothesis** — and `IsInvertible` gives no way to
74	  produce it for the sheaf. So the chicken-and-egg is real: bespoke, Mathlib-scale. **Matches
75	  iter-245's NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL exactly; off the A.1.c path.**
76	- **CHEAP half — `IsLocallyTrivial M ⟹ coherent loc-free rank 1`.** Once `M` is loc-triv,
77	  on each chart `M.restrict U.ι ≅ 𝒪_U` (rank-1 free, definitional from
78	  `LineBundlePullback.lean:115`). Rank 1 is then free, flatness is free (`𝒪_U` flat), and
79	  "coherent" = `SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation` is assembled by handing the trivialising
80	  cover to `QuasicoherentData` with each chart's presentation = the trivial free presentation of
81	  `𝒪_U`. No spreading-out, no stalks, no Mathlib gap.
82	- **THE COST-COLLAPSING FACT.** The Quot embedding's input is NOT a bare `IsInvertible` object —
83	  it is a point of `Pic⁰_{C/k}`, the representing scheme of RPF, whose carrier is
84	  **`IsLocallyTrivial`** (`OnProduct = {M // IsLocallyTrivial M}`, STRATEGY "RPF carrier =
85	  IsLocallyTrivial (DECIDED on merits)"; `exists_tensorObj_inverse` already returns a loc-triv
86	  witness). So the embedding never has to cross the HARD half: it starts already loc-triv.
87	- **Gap**: the literal `IsInvertible ⟹ …` is divergent-with-cost (Mathlib-scale); the
88	  loc-triv-routed `IsLocallyTrivial ⟹ …` is divergent-equivalent-but-cheap.
89	- **Verdict**: **DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY** — do NOT build the literal `IsInvertible` statement.
90	  Build `IsLocallyTrivial ⟹ IsFinitePresentation (+ rank-1 record)` and feed the embedding from
91	  the loc-triv carrier. The deepest-unquantified-risk framing dissolves: it is the SAME hard
92	  spreading-out as the forward bridge **only if you insist on the `IsInvertible` entry point**,
93	  which the consumer does not require.
94	
95	### Decision 3: cost band + build sketch (Q3)
96	
97	- **If forced from `IsInvertible`** (NOT recommended): same Mathlib-scale bespoke spreading-out
98	  as iter-245's forward bridge — order ~300–600 LOC of genuinely new sheaf finite-presentation
99	  spreading-out, or pin with a typed `sorry`. Budget as Mathlib-scale.
100	- **From `IsLocallyTrivial`** (recommended; the real cost): **~120–250 LOC / ~3–6 iters.**
101	  Bulk is bookkeeping, not deep mathematics.
102	  - **C1 — cover extraction** (~30–60 LOC): turn the pointwise `IsLocallyTrivial M`
103	    (`∀x ∃ affine U, M|_U ≅ 𝒪_U`) into an indexed affine cover + per-index trivialisation iso.
104	    Watch: the `J.over X` site instances `QuasicoherentData` demands
105	    (`HasWeakSheafify`/`WEqualsLocallyBijective`/`HasSheafCompose`) must be discharged for
106	    `X.ringCatSheaf`; the project already inhabits the `SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf` world, so
107	    these should resolve, but confirm at build entry (chief unknown).
108	  - **C2 — per-chart free presentation** (~30–60 LOC): from `M|_U ≅ 𝒪_U = free {*}` produce
109	    `(M|_U).Presentation` / `QuasicoherentData.presentation i` via `SheafOfModules.free` +
110	    `ιFree`; the presentation of a rank-1 free is trivial.
111	  - **C3 — assemble `M.IsFinitePresentation`** (~30–60 LOC): package C1+C2 into
112	    `SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData` + `IsFinitePresentation.mk`; `IsFiniteType` is then free
113	    (`instIsFiniteTypeOfIsFinitePresentation`).
114	  - **C4 — rank-1 / flat records + Quot-point wrapper** (~20–40 LOC): rank 1 and flatness are
115	    chart-local (`𝒪_U`); expose whatever the embedding consumes (Hilbert-poly input / coherent
116	    quotient datum).
117	- **Named gap that must be built first**: none Mathlib-scale on the loc-triv route — the only
118	  bespoke piece is C1's cover-to-`QuasicoherentData` adaptor, which is project-local plumbing,
119	  not a missing Mathlib theorem. The Mathlib-scale gap (sheaf finite-presentation spreading-out)
120	  is needed ONLY on the abandoned `IsInvertible` entry point.
121	
122	## Recommendation
123	Re-estimate the A.2.c "Quot embedding line-bundle coherence" row at **~120–250 LOC / ~3–6 iters,
124	NOT Mathlib-scale**, on the firm condition that the embedding is fed from the `IsLocallyTrivial`
125	carrier (the RPF/Pic⁰ representing-object carrier) rather than from bare `IsInvertible`. The
126	project should build `IsLocallyTrivial M ⟹ M.IsFinitePresentation` (Mathlib
127	`SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation` via `QuasicoherentData` from the trivialising cover) plus a
128	rank-1/flat record, and NOT build `IsInvertible ⟹ IsLocallyTrivial` (the Mathlib-scale
129	finite-presentation spreading-out from iter-245, which is off-path). An engine prover lane on the
130	loc-triv coherence half can safely run in parallel with the substrate — its only nontrivial
131	dependency is the `J.over X` site instances for `X.ringCatSheaf`, which should be checked in the
132	lane's first iter (the chief de-risking step). Do NOT open a lane on the `IsInvertible` entry
133	point. The strategy critic's "deepest unquantified risk" is real for the literal statement but is
134	retired by the carrier choice the project already made.

55	- **Mathlib idiom**: invertibility ⟹ locally free of rank 1 lives ONLY at the

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.2.b sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
8	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) -- a projective \(S\)-scheme representing the
9	% functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) with
10	% Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\) on every fiber. The Quot construction is the
11	% \emph{existence engine} behind the relative Picard functor: A.2.c
12	% (\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) wires this chapter together with
13	% the relative Picard functor (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}, A.1.c)
14	% through the Abel map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\), which factors
15	% through the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\) -- the
16	% case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor. This chapter is gated on
17	% A.2.a (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}, written this iter) and
18	% A.1.a (\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}, on disk).
19	%
20	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The construction embeds Quot as a
21	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
22	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
23	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
24	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
25	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
26	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
27	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability; it is a
28	% candidate for promotion to its own chapter
29	% \texttt{Picard\_GrassmannianScheme.tex} (writer iter-175+) -- see "Notes
30	% for Plan Agent" in the writer report.
31	
32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:quot_setup}
34	
35	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
36	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
37	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the

2079	\begin{definition}
2080	\label{definition-locally-free}
2081	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
2082	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
2083	\begin{enumerate}
2084	\item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it locally free} if for every
2085	point $x \in X$ there exist a set $I$ and an open
2086	neighbourhood $x \in U \subset X$
2087	such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$ is isomorphic to
2088	$\bigoplus_{i \in I} \mathcal{O}_X|_U$ as an $\mathcal{O}_X|_U$-module.
2089	\item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it finite locally free} if we may
2090	choose the index sets $I$ to be finite.
2091	\item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it finite locally free of rank $r$}
2092	if we may choose the index sets $I$ to have cardinality $r$.
2093	\end{enumerate}
2094	\end{definition}
2095	
2096	\noindent
2097	A finite direct sum of (finite) locally free sheaves is (finite)
2098	locally free. However, it may not be the case that an infinite direct
2099	sum of locally free sheaves is locally free.

2105	If $\mathcal{F}$ is locally free then it is quasi-coherent.
2106	\end{lemma}

2115	be a morphism of ringed spaces. If $\mathcal{G}$ is
2116	a locally free $\mathcal{O}_Y$-module, then

2124	\begin{lemma}
2125	\label{lemma-rank}
2126	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
2127	Suppose that the support of $\mathcal{O}_X$ is $X$,
2128	i.e., all stalks of $\mathcal{O}_X$ are nonzero rings.
2129	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a locally free sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
2130	There exists a locally constant function
2131	$$
2132	\text{rank}_\mathcal{F} :
2133	X \longrightarrow \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}\cup\{\infty\}
2134	$$
2135	such that for any point $x \in X$ the cardinality of any
2136	set $I$ such that $\mathcal{F}$ is isomorphic to
2137	$\bigoplus_{i\in I} \mathcal{O}_X$ in a neighbourhood
2138	of $x$ is $\text{rank}_\mathcal{F}(x)$.
2139	\end{lemma}
2140	
2141	\begin{proof}
2142	Under the assumption of the lemma the cardinality of $I$ can be read off
2143	from the rank of the free module $\mathcal{F}_x$ over the nonzero ring
2144	$\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$, and it is constant in a neighbourhood of $x$.
2145	\end{proof}

2147	\begin{lemma}
2148	\label{lemma-map-finite-locally-free}
2149	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Let $r \geq 0$.
2150	Let $\varphi : \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G}$ be a map of finite
2151	locally free $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules of rank $r$.
2152	Then $\varphi$ is an isomorphism if and only if $\varphi$
2153	is surjective.
2154	\end{lemma}
2155	
2156	\begin{proof}
2157	Assume $\varphi$ is surjective. Pick $x \in X$.
2158	There exists an open neighbourhood $U$ of $x$
2159	such that both $\mathcal{F}|_U$ and $\mathcal{G}|_U$ are
2160	isomorphic to $\mathcal{O}_U^{\oplus r}$.
2161	Pick lifts of the free generators of $\mathcal{G}|_U$
2162	to obtain a map $\psi : \mathcal{G}|_U \to \mathcal{F}|_U$
2163	such that $\varphi|_U \circ \psi = \text{id}$.
2164	Hence we conclude that the map
2165	$\Gamma(U, \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma(U, \mathcal{G})$
2166	induced by $\varphi$ is surjective. Since both
2167	$\Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})$ and $\Gamma(U, \mathcal{G})$
2168	are isomorphic to $\Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_U)^{\oplus r}$
2169	as an $\Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_U)$-module we may
2170	apply Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-fun} to see that
2171	$\Gamma(U, \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma(U, \mathcal{G})$
2172	is injective. This finishes the proof.
2173	\end{proof}

2176	\label{lemma-direct-summand-of-locally-free-is-locally-free}
2177	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$
2178	are local rings, then any direct summand of a finite locally free
2179	$\mathcal{O}_X$-module is finite locally free.

2181

2184	$\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{H}$.
2185	Let $x \in X$ be a point. Then $\mathcal{H}_x$ is a finite free
2186	$\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$-module.

2193	$\mathcal{H}$.)

4041	\noindent
4042	Similarly to the case of modules over rings
4043	(More on Algebra, Section \ref{more-algebra-section-picard})
4044	we have the following definition.
4045	
4046	\begin{definition}
4047	\label{definition-invertible}
4048	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. An
4049	{\it invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module} is a sheaf
4050	of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{L}$ such that
4051	the functor
4052	$$
4053	\textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \longrightarrow \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X),\quad
4054	\mathcal{F} \longmapsto \mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}
4055	$$
4056	is an equivalence of categories. We say that $\mathcal{L}$ is
4057	{\it trivial} if it is isomorphic as an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
4058	to $\mathcal{O}_X$.
4059	\end{definition}
4060	
4061	\noindent
4062	Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}
4063	below explains the relationship with locally free modules
4064	of rank $1$.
4065	
4066	\begin{lemma}
4067	\label{lemma-invertible}
4068	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Let $\mathcal{L}$
4069	be an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. Equivalent are
4070	\begin{enumerate}
4071	\item $\mathcal{L}$ is invertible, and
4072	\item there exists an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{N}$
4073	such that
4074	$\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X$.
4075	\end{enumerate}
4076	In this case $\mathcal{L}$ is locally a direct summand of a finite free
4077	$\mathcal{O}_X$-module and the module $\mathcal{N}$ in (2) is isomorphic to
4078	$\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$.
4079	\end{lemma}
4080	
4081	\begin{proof}
4082	Assume (1). Then the functor $- \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$
4083	is essentially surjective, hence there exists an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
4084	$\mathcal{N}$ as in (2). If (2) holds, then the functor
4085	$- \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N}$ is a quasi-inverse
4086	to the functor $- \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$ and
4087	we see that (1) holds.
4088	
4089	\medskip\noindent
4090	Assume (1) and (2) hold. Denote
4091	$\psi : \mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N} \to \mathcal{O}_X$
4092	the given isomorphism. Let $x \in X$. Choose an open neighbourhood
4093	$U$ an integer $n \geq 1$ and sections $s_i \in \mathcal{L}(U)$,
4094	$t_i \in \mathcal{N}(U)$ such that $\psi(\sum s_i \otimes t_i) = 1$.
4095	Consider the isomorphisms
4096	$$
4097	\mathcal{L}|_U \to
4098	\mathcal{L}|_U \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_U}
4099	\mathcal{L}|_U \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_U} \mathcal{N}|_U \to \mathcal{L}|_U
4100	$$
4101	where the first arrow sends $s$ to $\sum s_i \otimes s \otimes t_i$
4102	and the second arrow sends $s \otimes s' \otimes t$ to $\psi(s' \otimes t)s$.
4103	We conclude that $s \mapsto \sum \psi(s \otimes t_i)s_i$ is
4104	an automorphism of $\mathcal{L}|_U$. This automorphism factors as
4105	$$
4106	\mathcal{L}|_U \to \mathcal{O}_U^{\oplus n} \to \mathcal{L}|_U
4107	$$
4108	where the first arrow is given by
4109	$s \mapsto (\psi(s \otimes t_1), \ldots, \psi(s \otimes t_n))$
4110	and the second arrow by $(a_1, \ldots, a_n) \mapsto \sum a_i s_i$.
4111	In this way we conclude that $\mathcal{L}|_U$ is a direct summand
4112	of a finite free $\mathcal{O}_U$-module.
4113	
4114	\medskip\noindent
4115	Assume (1) and (2) hold. Consider the evaluation map
4116	$$
4117	\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
4118	\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)
4119	\longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_X
4120	$$
4121	To finish the proof of the lemma
4122	we will show this is an isomorphism by checking it induces
4123	isomorphisms on stalks. Let $x \in X$.
4124	Since we know (by the previous paragraph)
4125	that $\mathcal{L}$ is a finitely presented
4126	$\mathcal{O}_X$-module
4127	we can use Lemma \ref{lemma-stalk-internal-hom}
4128	to see that it suffices to show that
4129	$$
4130	\mathcal{L}_x \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X, x}}
4131	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_{X, x}}(\mathcal{L}_x, \mathcal{O}_{X, x})
4132	\longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{X, x}
4133	$$
4134	is an isomorphism. Since
4135	$\mathcal{L}_x \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X, x}} \mathcal{N}_x =
4136	(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N})_x =
4137	\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ (Lemma \ref{lemma-stalk-tensor-product})
4138	the desired result follows from
4139	More on Algebra, Lemma \ref{more-algebra-lemma-invertible}.
4140	\end{proof}

4142	\begin{lemma}
4143	\label{lemma-pullback-invertible}
4144	Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a
4145	morphism of ringed spaces. The pullback $f^*\mathcal{L}$ of an
4146	invertible $\mathcal{O}_Y$-module is invertible.
4147	\end{lemma}
4148	
4149	\begin{proof}
4150	By Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible}
4151	there exists an $\mathcal{O}_Y$-module $\mathcal{N}$ such that
4152	$\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_Y} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_Y$.
4153	Pulling back we get
4154	$f^*\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} f^*\mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X$
4155	by Lemma \ref{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}.
4156	Thus $f^*\mathcal{L}$ is invertible by Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible}.
4157	\end{proof}

4159	\begin{lemma}
4160	\label{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}
4161	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
4162	$\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
4163	If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
4164	the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case).
4165	\end{lemma}
4166	
4167	\begin{proof}
4168	The parenthetical statement follows by considering a one point
4169	space $X$ with sheaf of rings $\mathcal{O}_X$ given by a ring $R$.
4170	Then invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules correspond to invertible
4171	$R$-modules, hence as soon as $\Pic(R)$ is not the trivial group,
4172	then we get an example.

4174	\medskip\noindent
4175	Assume $\mathcal{L}$ is locally free of rank $1$ and consider the
4176	evaluation map
4177	$$
4178	\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
4179	\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)
4180	\longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_X
4181	$$
4182	Looking over an open covering trivialization $\mathcal{L}$, we see
4183	that this map is an isomorphism. Hence $\mathcal{L}$ is invertible
4184	by Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible}.
4185	
4186	\medskip\noindent
4187	Assume all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings and $\mathcal{L}$
4188	invertible. In the proof of Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible}
4189	we have seen that $\mathcal{L}_x$ is an invertible
4190	$\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$-module for all $x \in X$. Since
4191	$\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ is local, we see that
4192	$\mathcal{L}_x \cong \mathcal{O}_{X, x}$
4193	(More on Algebra, Section \ref{more-algebra-section-picard}).
4194	Since $\mathcal{L}$ is of finite presentation by
4195	Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible} we conclude that $\mathcal{L}$
4196	is locally free of rank $1$ by
4197	Lemma \ref{lemma-finite-presentation-stalk-free}.
4198	\end{proof}

1375	Here is the definition.
1376	
1377	\begin{definition}
1378	\label{definition-finite-presentation}
1379	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1380	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
1381	We say that $\mathcal{F}$ is of {\it finite presentation}
1382	if for every point $x \in X$ there exists an open neighbourhood
1383	$x\in U \subset X$, and  $n, m \in \mathbf{N}$ such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$
1384	is isomorphic to the cokernel of a map
1385	$$
1386	\bigoplus\nolimits_{j = 1, \ldots, m}
1387	\mathcal{O}_U
1388	\longrightarrow
1389	\bigoplus\nolimits_{i = 1, \ldots, n}
1390	\mathcal{O}_U
1391	$$
1392	\end{definition}
1393	
1394	\noindent
1395	This means that $X$ is covered by open sets $U$
1396	such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$ has a {\it presentation}
1397	of the form
1398	$$
1399	\bigoplus\nolimits_{j = 1, \ldots, m}
1400	\mathcal{O}_U
1401	\longrightarrow
1402	\bigoplus\nolimits_{i = 1, \ldots, n}
1403	\mathcal{O}_U
1404	\to
1405	\mathcal{F}|_U
1406	\to
1407	0.
1408	$$
1409	Here presentation signifies that the displayed
1410	sequence is exact. In other words
1411	\begin{enumerate}
1412	\item for every point $x$ of $X$ there exists
1413	an open neighbourhood such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$
1414	is generated by finitely many global sections, and
1415	\item for a suitable choice of these sections
1416	the kernel of the associated surjection is also
1417	generated by finitely many global sections.
1418	\end{enumerate}

1	\chapter{Line-bundle pullback on a relative curve}
2	\label{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-173). This chapter is the A.1.b sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the line-bundle
7	% pullback functor \(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) along the
8	% projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) of a relative curve, and the
9	% set-valued relative Picard presheaf
10	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) := \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\)
11	% as a functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\). This chapter is gated on
12	% \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (iter-172) for the relative-spectrum machinery
13	% that any concrete Lean-side affine-cover argument will need, and it itself
14	% gates A.1.c (\'etale sheafification / \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) as a sheaf), A.2.*
15	% (Quot / Hilbert representability), A.3 (identity component), and A.4
16	% (universal property = Albanese / Jacobian).
17	
18	% NOTE (planner directive iter-173). Per the iter-173 blueprint-review
19	% recommendation (see .archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-route173.md,
20	% sub-phase choice), this chapter defines \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) as a
21	% \emph{set-valued} presheaf on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\). The abelian-group refinement
22	% (and the \'etale-sheafification step) belong to the follow-up chapter
23	% Picard_RelPicFunctor.tex (A.1.c). The set-valued formulation lets us discharge
24	% well-definedness of the quotient and functoriality of \(g^*\) as soon as a
25	% line-bundle pullback functor and its composition identity are in hand, with
26	% no reliance on Mathlib's \texttt{AddCommGrp} \'etale-sheafification API which
27	% does not yet exist.
28	
29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in \(C\) and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a \(k\)-scheme \(T\); the relative curve over \(T\) is
37	\(C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T\), with \(\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C\) the
38	companion projection. Both \(\pi_T\) and \(\pi_C\) are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and \(C/k\) is flat since \(k\) is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
43	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
44	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54	
55	\section{Line bundles on a relative curve}
56	\label{sec:lbp_line_bundle}
57	
58	\begin{definition}\leanok
59	[Line bundle on a product]
60	  \label{def:line_bundle_on_product}
61	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}

\chapter{Coherence of locally trivial line bundles}
\label{chap:Picard_LineBundleCoherence}
% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundleCoherence.lean

% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter supplies the cheap, on-path half of the
% line-bundle coherence requirement of the A.2.c Quot-scheme embedding
% (\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}).
% The Quot functor takes coherent quotients of a coherent sheaf; to feed it a
% line bundle one must know that the line bundle is itself coherent, i.e. a
% finitely presented \(\mathcal{O}\)-module. The embedding's input is a point of
% the relative Picard scheme \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), whose carrier is the
% \emph{locally trivial} predicate \(\IsLocallyTrivial\) of
% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (the project-side stand-in for an
% invertible sheaf, a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules locally isomorphic to
% \(\mathcal{O}\) of rank one). The chapter proves
% \(\IsLocallyTrivial M \Rightarrow M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\),
% together with a rank-one / flatness record. The general implication
% ``\(M\) tensor-invertible \(\Rightarrow\) \(M\) locally free of rank one''
% (the converse spreading-out, Stacks Lemma~0B8M when stalks are local) is
% \emph{not} crossed here: the carrier is already locally trivial, so the
% finite-presentation spreading-out is never needed.

\section{Setup and motivation}
\label{sec:lbc_setup}

Fix a scheme \(X\) and write \(X.\mathtt{Modules}\) for the category of sheaves
of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) on the
ringed-space site of \(X\), with structure sheaf \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)).
A \emph{line bundle} on \(X\) is an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module; in the
sense of \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} we model line bundles by the
predicate \(\IsLocallyTrivial\): a module \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) is locally
trivial of rank one if every point \(x \in X\) has an \emph{affine} open
neighbourhood \(U\) on which the restriction \(M|_U\) is isomorphic, as an
\(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module, to the structure sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U}\):
\[
  \IsLocallyTrivial(M) \;:=\;
  \forall x \in X,\ \exists\, U \ni x \text{ affine open},\quad
  M|_U \;\cong\; \struct{U}.
\]
This is the project-side substitute for Stacks Definition~17.25.1
(Tag~01CS, an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module) restricted to rank one, and
agrees with the classical ``locally free of rank one'' description by Stacks
Lemma~17.25.4 (Tag~0B8M).

The goal of the chapter is to record that such a module is \emph{coherent} in the
sense the Quot construction consumes, namely finitely presented:
\(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) in Mathlib's
\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\) (built from a
\(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\)). The mathematics is elementary: on each
trivialising chart \(M|_U \cong \struct{U}\) is finite free of rank one, hence
has the trivial finite presentation, and finite presentation is by definition a
local condition. No stalk computation and no spreading-out are involved. The
content is the bookkeeping that turns the pointwise trivialisation into the
indexed \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) Mathlib expects.

\section{Site instances for quasi-coherent data}
\label{sec:lbc_site_instances}

% NOTE (chief unknown). Mathlib's SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation /
% QuasicoherentData are stated under three site hypotheses on the slice
% topologies J.over X of the underlying site J of X.ringCatSheaf:
%   [∀ x, (J.over x).HasSheafCompose (forget₂ RingCat AddCommGrpCat)]
%   [∀ x, HasWeakSheafify (J.over x) AddCommGrpCat]
%   [∀ x, (J.over x).WEqualsLocallyBijective AddCommGrpCat]
% These must be discharged (or assumed as instances) before the statements in
% this chapter typecheck for X.ringCatSheaf. The project already lives in the
% SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf world, so these should resolve; this is the one
% item to verify when the Lean file is scaffolded.

The Mathlib predicates \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\) and
\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData}\) are stated relative to a
Grothendieck topology \(J\) on a base category \(C\) and a sheaf of rings
\(R : \Sheaf(J, \RingCat)\). They require, for every object \(x : C\), three
instances on the slice topology \(J/x\) (Mathlib \(J.\mathtt{over}\ x\)):
\[
  (J/x).\HasSheafCompose(\forget_{\RingCat \to \AddCommGrpCat}),\quad
  \HasWeakSheafify(J/x, \AddCommGrpCat),\quad
  (J/x).\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}(\AddCommGrpCat).
\]
For \(X.\mathtt{Modules} = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\ X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
these are the ambient site instances of the ringed space underlying \(X\). We
record their availability as a standing assumption for the chapter; verifying it
for \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is the single instance-resolution check the
formalisation must clear at entry, and is independent of the mathematical
content below.

\begin{assumption}
  \label{ass:lbc_site_instances}
  For the ringed-space site underlying \(X\) with sheaf of rings
  \(R = X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\), every slice topology \(J/x\) carries the
  three instances \(\HasSheafCompose\), \(\HasWeakSheafify\) and
  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) (for the forgetful functor
  \(\RingCat \to \AddCommGrpCat\)) demanded by
  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData}\). Under this assumption the
  predicate \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) is well-formed for every
  \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\).
\end{assumption}

\section{From pointwise triviality to a trivialising cover}
\label{sec:lbc_cover}

\begin{lemma}[Trivialising affine cover]
  \label{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.exists_trivializing_cover}
  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Definition 17.25.1
  % "invertible module" (Tag 01CS), Section 17.25 (Tag 01CR)
  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4046-L4059).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. An
  %  {\it invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module} is a sheaf
  %  of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{L}$ such that
  %  the functor
  %  $$
  %  \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \longrightarrow \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X),\quad
  %  \mathcal{F} \longmapsto \mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}
  %  $$
  %  is an equivalence of categories. We say that $\mathcal{L}$ is
  %  {\it trivial} if it is isomorphic as an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
  %  to $\mathcal{O}_X$."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CS (Modules, Definition~17.25.1).}
  Let \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial, \(\IsLocallyTrivial(M)\).
  Then there exist an index set \(I\), a family of affine open subschemes
  \(\{U_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(X\) covering \(X\) (i.e. \(\bigcup_i U_i = X\)), and
  for each \(i\) an isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules
  \[
    e_i \;:\; M|_{U_i} \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \struct{U_i}
  \]
  to the structure sheaf-as-module on \(U_i\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product}
  Apply the hypothesis \(\IsLocallyTrivial(M)\) at every point \(x \in X\): it
  produces an affine open \(U_x \ni x\) and an isomorphism
  \(M|_{U_x} \cong \struct{U_x}\). Take \(I := X\) (the underlying set of points),
  \(U_i := U_i\) the chosen neighbourhood and \(e_i\) the chosen isomorphism.
  The family \(\{U_x\}_{x \in X}\) covers \(X\) because each \(x\) lies in its own
  \(U_x\). This is exactly the data the definition supplies, packaged as an
  indexed family rather than a pointwise existential.
\end{proof}

\section{The trivial presentation on a chart}
\label{sec:lbc_chart_presentation}

\begin{lemma}[Finite free presentation on a chart]
  \label{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation}
  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Definition 17.10.1
  % "module of finite presentation" (Section 17.11, finite presentation)
  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L1377-L1392).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "We say that $\mathcal{F}$ is of {\it finite presentation}
  %  if for every point $x \in X$ there exists an open neighbourhood
  %  $x\in U \subset X$, and  $n, m \in \mathbf{N}$ such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$
  %  is isomorphic to the cokernel of a map
  %  $$
  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{j = 1, \ldots, m}
  %  \mathcal{O}_U
  %  \longrightarrow
  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{i = 1, \ldots, n}
  %  \mathcal{O}_U
  %  $$"
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules, Section~17.11
  (finite presentation).}
  On each chart \(U_i\) of the trivialising cover of
  \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}, the restriction \(M|_{U_i}\) admits the
  trivial finite free presentation
  \[
    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \struct{U_i} \;\longrightarrow\; M|_{U_i}
    \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
  \]
  i.e. \(M|_{U_i}\) is the cokernel of the zero map
  \(\bigoplus_{j \in \varnothing} \struct{U_i} \to \struct{U_i}\)
  (the case \(m = 0\), \(n = 1\) of the finite-presentation pattern). In Mathlib
  terms, the unit sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i} = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\)
  carries the canonical free presentation built from
  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.free}\) on the one-element generating family with
  \(\mathtt{ier}\) and no relations, and this presentation is finite.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
  The structure sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i}\) is free of rank one on the
  single generator \(1\): the identity map
  \(\struct{U_i} \to \struct{U_i}\) exhibits it as
  \(\bigoplus_{i \in \{*\}} \struct{U_i}\), so the surjection
  \(\struct{U_i}^{\oplus 1} \twoheadrightarrow \struct{U_i}\) is an isomorphism
  and its kernel is zero, generated by the empty family. Thus
  \(\struct{U_i}\) has a presentation with \(n = 1\) generators and \(m = 0\)
  relations; this presentation is finite. Transporting it along the
  trivialisation \(e_i : M|_{U_i} \xrightarrow{\sim} \struct{U_i}\) of
  \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover} (presentations are stable under
  isomorphism of the presented module) yields a finite free presentation of
  \(M|_{U_i}\).
\end{proof}

\section{Finite presentation of a locally trivial line bundle}
\label{sec:lbc_finite_presentation}

\begin{theorem}[Locally trivial \(\Rightarrow\) finitely presented]
  \label{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.isFinitePresentation}
  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover, lem:lbc_chart_presentation, ass:lbc_site_instances}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces,
  % Lemma 17.25.4 (Tag 0B8M), invertible vs locally free of rank one
  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4159-L4165).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
  %  $\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
  %  If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
  %  the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case)."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~0B8M (Modules, Lemma~17.25.4).}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying \cref{ass:lbc_site_instances} and let
  \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial, \(\IsLocallyTrivial(M)\). Then
  \(M\) is finitely presented: \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) holds in
  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\).
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover, lem:lbc_chart_presentation}
  By \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover} choose a trivialising affine cover
  \(\{U_i\}_{i \in I}\) with isomorphisms \(e_i : M|_{U_i} \cong \struct{U_i}\).
  Assemble these into a \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) for \(M\): the index type
  is \(I\), the covering objects are the affine opens \(U_i\) (which cover \(X\)),
  and the presentation attached to index \(i\) is the finite free presentation of
  \(M|_{U_i}\) (equivalently, of the restriction \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U_i\))
  produced in \cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}. Each such presentation is
  finite, so the resulting quasi-coherent datum is a
  \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData.IsFinitePresentation}\). Feeding it to
  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation.mk}\) gives
  \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\).

  The only non-formal input is the trivialising cover; everything else is the
  definitional unwinding of finite presentation as a local property
  (Stacks Definition~17.11.1), since a rank-one free module is its own finite
  presentation. No stalk computation and no neighbourhood-spreading argument is
  used: the hypothesis already provides the local free models.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Locally trivial \(\Rightarrow\) finite type and quasi-coherent]
  \label{cor:lbc_isFiniteType}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.isFiniteType}
  \uses{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
  Under the hypotheses of \cref{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}, the locally
  trivial module \(M\) is also of finite type
  (\(M.\mathtt{IsFiniteType}\)) and quasi-coherent
  (\(M.\mathtt{IsQuasicoherent}\)).
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
  Finite presentation implies finite type by Mathlib's
  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsFiniteTypeOfIsFinitePresentation}\), and implies
  quasi-coherence by
  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsQuasicoherentOfIsFinitePresentation}\); both are
  immediate from \cref{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}.
\end{proof}

\section{Rank one and flatness record}
\label{sec:lbc_rank_flat}

% NOTE. Mathlib (at the pin) has no SheafOfModules-level "locally free of rank r"
% or "flat" predicate (verified absent: no SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFree). The
% rank-one and flatness facts are therefore recorded chart-locally, as the data
% the Quot embedding actually consumes, rather than as instances of a missing
% global predicate. Project-side predicates may be introduced if a downstream
% consumer needs them packaged; this is flagged for the plan agent.

\begin{lemma}[Chart-local rank one and flatness]
  \label{lem:lbc_rank_flat}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.chart_free_rank_one}
  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces,
  % Definition 17.14.1 "(finite) locally free of rank $r$" (Section 17.14)
  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2079-L2094).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it locally free} if for every
  %  point $x \in X$ there exist a set $I$ and an open
  %  neighbourhood $x \in U \subset X$
  %  such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$ is isomorphic to
  %  $\bigoplus_{i \in I} \mathcal{O}_X|_U$ as an $\mathcal{O}_X|_U$-module.
  %  We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it finite locally free} if we may
  %  choose the index sets $I$ to be finite.
  %  We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it finite locally free of rank $r$}
  %  if we may choose the index sets $I$ to have cardinality $r$."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules, Section~17.14 (finite locally
  free of rank \(r\)).}
  Let \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial. On each chart \(U_i\) of the
  trivialising cover of \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover} the restriction
  \(M|_{U_i} \cong \struct{U_i}\) is free of rank one over
  \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\); in particular it is flat, because \(\struct{U_i}\) is a
  flat \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-module (a ring is a flat module over itself, and
  flatness is preserved by the isomorphism \(e_i\)). Consequently \(M\) is, in
  the sense of \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, finite locally free of rank one
  and flat: these properties hold on the cover \(\{U_i\}\) by construction.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
  Immediate from the trivialisation: on \(U_i\), \(e_i\) identifies \(M|_{U_i}\)
  with \(\struct{U_i} = \bigoplus_{i \in \{*\}} \struct{U_i}\), the free module
  of rank one. The structure sheaf-as-module is flat over itself, and an
  isomorphic module is flat; the index set \(\{*\}\) has cardinality one, so the
  rank is one. The properties are stated on the chart \(U_i\) because, at the
  pinned Mathlib commit, there is no \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\)-level locally
  free or flat predicate to instantiate globally; the chart-local statements are
  what the Quot-scheme embedding consumes when it forms the Hilbert-polynomial
  input on a fibre.
\end{proof}

169	  \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}, the restriction \(M|_{U_i}\) admits the
170	  trivial finite free presentation
171	  \[
172	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \struct{U_i} \;\longrightarrow\; M|_{U_i}
173	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
174	  \]
175	  i.e. \(M|_{U_i}\) is the cokernel of the zero map
176	  \(\bigoplus_{j \in \varnothing} \struct{U_i} \to \struct{U_i}\)
177	  (the case \(m = 0\), \(n = 1\) of the finite-presentation pattern). In Mathlib
178	  terms, the unit sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i} = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\)
179	  carries the canonical free presentation built from
180	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.free}\) on the one-element generating family,
181	  together with its canonical map out of the free module, and no relations;
182	  this presentation is finite.
183	\end{lemma}

185	\begin{proof}
186	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
187	  The structure sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i}\) is free of rank one on the
188	  single generator \(1\): the identity map
189	  \(\struct{U_i} \to \struct{U_i}\) exhibits it as
190	  \(\bigoplus_{i \in \{*\}} \struct{U_i}\), so the surjection
191	  \(\struct{U_i}^{\oplus 1} \twoheadrightarrow \struct{U_i}\) is an isomorphism
192	  and its kernel is zero, generated by the empty family. Thus
193	  \(\struct{U_i}\) has a presentation with \(n = 1\) generators and \(m = 0\)
194	  relations; this presentation is finite. Transporting it along the
195	  trivialisation \(e_i : M|_{U_i} \xrightarrow{\sim} \struct{U_i}\) of
196	  \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover} (presentations are stable under
197	  isomorphism of the presented module) yields a finite free presentation of
